nativeRange.habitat: Slow-moving blackwater streams, peat swamps, and forest-shaded rivers with soft, acidic, tannin-stained water; typically over substrates of leaf litter and sandy sediment

decorPreferences: leaf litter (Indian almond leaves, oak leaves) to acidify water and provide cover
driftwood and bogwood for tannin release and hiding spots
dim lighting or floating plants to diffuse light
dark substrate (sand or fine gravel) to enhance coloration
dense planting with low-light species (Java fern, Cryptocoryne, Microsorum)

diet.notes: Accepts a wide range of small foods. High-quality micro-pellets and fine flake form the staple diet; supplement regularly with small frozen or live foods such as daphnia, micro-worms, and baby brine shrimp to maintain condition and encourage spawning. Vegetable matter (spirulina flake, blanched spinach) also accepted. Naturally feeds on small invertebrates, algae, and organic detritus in the wild.

goodWith: other small blackwater fish (e.g., ember tetras, dwarf rasboras, celestial pearl danio)
small Apistogramma (with caution — monitor for aggression)
avoidWith: fin-nipping companions that may outcompete for food
Banded Barb (Desmopuntius johorensis) — easily confused in trade but distinct species with different requirements

imageCandidates.notes: Photographer: anonymous (iNaturalist, also on Wikimedia Commons). 800x600. Sharp aquarium shot over sand showing the whole fish with vibrant golden-amber body, five dark vertical bars, and orange-red fins - all key diagnostic features in full colour.

Photographer: Rupert A. Collins. 639x391. Crisp lateral specimen shot on a measuring board; full colour with clear dark vertical bars on a golden body and orange fins - strong ID reference, but lab background is less display-worthy than the aquarium shot.

summary: The Fiveband Barb (Desmopuntius hexazona) is a small blackwater specialist from the Malay Peninsula and Borneo, recognisable by five prominent dark vertical bars on a golden-amber body. Despite the scientific name meaning 'six-banded,' hobbyists typically count five visible bars. It requires soft, acidic water and is best suited to intermediate keepers.
careNotes: The Fiveband Barb is a Borneo and Malay Peninsula specialist demanding soft, acidic blackwater conditions that most beginners do not maintain. Water pH should be kept between 4.0 and 6.5 with very low hardness (gH 1–8); using RO or rainwater blended with blackwater extract or Indian almond leaf infusion is strongly recommended. Standard tap water in hard-water areas is unsuitable without significant treatment. Temperature in the 23–28 °C range is appropriate; avoid the higher end long-term. The species epithet hexazona means 'six-banded,' but hobbyists typically count five prominent dark bars — the discrepancy arises because one bar (near the caudal peduncle) is often faint or partially obscured. This species is frequently confused in the trade with the Banded Barb (Desmopuntius johorensis), which is larger and has a different banding pattern; verify species identity at purchase. Keep in groups of eight or more to distribute any intraspecific chasing and to bring out natural schooling behaviour. Subdued lighting, leaf litter, and dark substrate significantly improve colour display and fish confidence. Provide regular small live or frozen foods to maintain peak health and breeding readiness. The genus Desmopuntius was established in 2013, separating several small Southeast Asian 'banded barbs' from the broader and paraphyletic Puntius grouping.
breedingNotes: Egg scatterers that will spawn among fine-leaved plants or over a mesh if conditions are optimal — very soft, acidic water with a pH of 5.0–6.0 and hardness kept very low. Condition the group on live and frozen foods. A small breeding tank with a moss mat or spawning mop over a mesh base is recommended; parents must be removed after spawning, as eggs and fry are not guarded. Eggs hatch in approximately 24–36 hours and fry become free-swimming within 3–5 days. First foods should be infusoria and vinegar eels, graduating to baby brine shrimp and micro-worms as the fry grow. Breeding difficulty is moderate, primarily because of the water-chemistry requirements.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Desmopuntius hexazona
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided native range (Malay Peninsula, Sumatra, Borneo), biotope (blackwater peat swamps), water parameters (pH 4.0–6.5, temperature 23–28°C, hardness 18–90 µS/cm conductivity equating to very soft), adult size (~5 cm), diet, compatibility, and breeding information. Also confirmed Kottelat 2013 reclassification from Puntius to Desmopuntius and confusion with D. johorensis.

additional.name: FishBase — Desmopuntius hexazona
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Cyprinidae, Cypriniformes), adult size (max 5 cm TL), native range (Malay Peninsula, Sumatra, Kalimantan/Borneo), and temperature range. Confirmed synonym Puntius hexazona (Regan, 1906) and current accepted name.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Barb Care and Species Overview
additional.notes: General barb care guidance used to cross-check diet, grouping recommendations, and temperament characterisation for small Southeast Asian barb species. Confirmed omnivore diet and shoaling requirements.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Desmopuntius hexazona species profile
additional.notes: Supplementary source for trade availability, identification versus D. johorensis, and hobbyist-level care notes. Confirmed blackwater specialist status and soft-acidic water requirement as the primary challenge.

fish.breedingNotes: The Fiveband Barb is an egg scatterer that spawns among fine-leaved plants or over a mesh in very soft, acidic water (pH 5.0–6.0). Parents must be removed after spawning. Eggs hatch in approximately 24–36 hours and fry become free-swimming within 3–5 days. First foods include infusoria and vinegar eels, followed by baby brine shrimp as the fry develop.
